coredata.dc:title: N-CQDs modified BiOBr with different nitrogen configurations synthesized from different precursors for efficient photocatalytic degradation of carbamazepine

Wang, Yaodong
Xie, Xiaoyun
Qi, Kemin

Dong, Wenrui
Wang, Zhaowei
coredata.dc:description: In the realm of persistent pharmaceutical contaminants photodegradation, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified bismuth-based photocatalysts have recently received a lot of interest. However, an in-depth understanding of the effect of both N chemical state and N chemical configuration on the photocatalytic activity was still lacking. Herein, three graphite/pyrrole N-doped CQDs with different contents were prepared by hydrothermal method to modify BiOBr (BNCs), respectively. The degradation kinetics of carbamazepine (CBZ) by BNC-eda (doped with 100 % pyrrole N, k = 0.363 min−1) was higher than that of BNC-trp (doped with 62 % pyrrole N and 38 % graphitic N, k = 0.099 min−1) and BNC-ur (doped with 58 % pyrrole N and 42 % graphitic N, k = 0.072 min−1) under visible-light irradiation. More importantly, the k of BNCs degrading CBZ showed a linear positive relationship with the percentage content of pyrrole-N in the corresponding N-CQDs with r2 = 0.9999. The characterization results showed that doping with pyrrole N rather than graphite N could be responsible for the increase in visible light absorption, the promotion of electron-hole separation and the generation of more superoxide radicals (•O2 –) and singlet oxygen (1O2) in BNCs. Toxicity Estimation Software Tool program showed that the BNC-eda exhibited CBZ toxicity reduction performance. Overall, this study offered a useful method for designing excellent N-CQDs modified Bi-based photocatalysts for the treatment of pharmaceutical wastewater.

head.ce:title: N-CQDs modified BiOBr with different nitrogen configurations synthesized from different precursors for efficient photocatalytic degradation of carbamazepine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the realm of persistent pharmaceutical contaminants photodegradation, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified bismuth-based photocatalysts have recently received a lot of interest. However, an in-depth understanding of the effect of both N chemical state and N chemical configuration on the photocatalytic activity was still lacking. Herein, three graphite/pyrrole N-doped CQDs with different contents were prepared by hydrothermal method to modify BiOBr (BNCs), respectively. The degradation kinetics of carbamazepine (CBZ) by BNC-eda (doped with 100 % pyrrole N, k = 0.363 min−1) was higher than that of BNC-trp (doped with 62 % pyrrole N and 38 % graphitic N, k = 0.099 min−1) and BNC-ur (doped with 58 % pyrrole N and 42 % graphitic N, k = 0.072 min−1) under visible-light irradiation. More importantly, the k of BNCs degrading CBZ showed a linear positive relationship with the percentage content of pyrrole-N in the corresponding N-CQDs with r2 = 0.9999. The characterization results showed that doping with pyrrole N rather than graphite N could be responsible for the increase in visible light absorption, the promotion of electron-hole separation and the generation of more superoxide radicals (•O2 –) and singlet oxygen (1O2) in BNCs. Toxicity Estimation Software Tool program showed that the BNC-eda exhibited CBZ toxicity reduction performance. Overall, this study offered a useful method for designing excellent N-CQDs modified Bi-based photocatalysts for the treatment of pharmaceutical wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the pharmaceutical industry has flourished, with increasing use in human and veterinary medicine, and pharmaceuticals have become a major type of emerging contaminants that could not be ignored [1,2]. Carbamazepine (CBZ) is a powerful anticonvulsant and neuropathic painkiller [3], poorly biodegradable and highly persistent in the aquatic environment [4,5]. Studies have shown that the concentration of CBZ commonly found in wastewater ranges from 1 to 3600 ng/m3, reaching 443 mg/m3 in pharmaceutical wastewater [6], and less than 10 % can be effectively removed by conventional methods [7]. A few works have investigated that CBZ residues in the aquatic environment cause adverse effects such as reproductive toxicity, developmental retardation and carcinogenicity to aquatic and terrestrial organisms (including bacteria, algae, invertebrates and fish) [8,9]. Even a small amount of CBZ in water is highly toxic to the human digestive and central nervous systems [10]. Therefore, much attention has been paid in recent years to finding a safe, environmentally friendly and effective method to remove CBZ from environmental waters [11].

As an advanced oxidation process (AOP) that can effectively address the drawbacks of conventional wastewater treatment technology, photocatalysis has been hailed as a potential method for the destruction of persistent organic contaminants in water [12,13]. Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, where X = Cl, Br, and I) are a unique ternary oxide semiconductor material that have an open layered structure made up of [Bi2O2]2+ layers sandwiched between two plates of halogen ions. [14–16]. Due to special electronic structure, they have recently attracted considerable attention with good photocatalytic activity and stability, as well as low cost and non-toxicity [17–19]. In comparison to BiOCl (∼3.2 eV) and BiOI (∼1.7 eV), BiOBr has a desirable band gap (∼2.6 eV) and visible-light response [20]. However, the large-scale uses of BiOBr nanoparticles are constrained by both the high complexation rate of photogenerated carriers and the susceptibility of nanoparticle aggregation [17,19,21]. In addition, much effort has been devoted to improving visible light harvesting and charge separation to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of carbon nanomaterials consisting of discrete quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles ≤ 10 nm in size. Due to their low toxicity and chemical stability, they have been widely used in adsorption, photovoltaics, and photocatalysis, among other applications [22–24]. In addition, CQDs are of increasing interest as a promising electron transfer mediator that can act as a co-catalyst for the formation of spatially separated centers of photogenerated charge carriers [25,26]. Bismuth-based Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs composite photocatalysts were prepared by photodeposition, and the removal of CBZ was up to 99.52 % within 2 h under visible-light irradiation [27]. However, the low photo-response efficiency and the limited internal electron transfer capacity of the CQDs have still hindered its further application [28]. According to the relevant studies, N doping on CQDs can not only reduce the band gap energy, but also induce charge delocalization because of the electron-rich nature of the N atom, leading to emission red shift and prolong the carrier lifetime [29,30]. In addition, dissolved O2 can be absorbed by the large number of oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs, which will facilitate the generation of radicals for the photocatalytic system [31,32]. Therefore, N-CQDs can absorb enough light and transfer charge effectively, which is expected to be realized in a photocatalytic system.

Recently, a number of studies have been carried out to synthesis N-doped carbon materials with different chemical states using different methods [33]. Research has demonstrated that pyridinic nitrogen in nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon generates oxygen reduction active sites under acidic conditions by designing N-doped carbon materials with different chemical states by high-temperature ammonia treatment and N-ion beam bombardment [34]. Graphene quantum dots with different N configurations on TiO2 surfaces were prepared by different reaction solvents (DI-water, tetrahydrofuran, butyraldehyde, and benzaldehyde), and it found that the production rate of active radicals of TiO2 was modulated, which significantly improved light trapping efficiency and charge separation of the photocatalysts [35]. NCQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 composites were prepared using urea as a precursor to achieve 98.7 % and 96.6 % removal of phenol and TC-HCl within 3 h and 5 min, respectively [36]. However, there is still a lack of in-depth knowledge and research on which N configuration in the synthesized N-CQDs modified catalysts is most conducive to improving photocatalytic performance in the field of removal of organic pollutants from water bodies. Therefore, in order to provide theoretical references for future studies on N-CQDs modified photocatalysts, it is necessary to clarify the type of N-doping and its role and mechanism in the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ in water.

Herein, graphite N/pyrrole N (57.9 %), graphite N/pyrrole N (62.2 %) and pyrrole N (100.0 %) doped CQDs modified BiOBr composite photocatalysts (BNCs) were prepared by hydrothermal methods using urea, L-tryptophan and ethylenediamine as nitrogen sources, respectively. Through a series of characterizations, the crystalline phase, morphology, light absorption and photoelectrochemical properties of these BNCs photocatalysts were systematically studied and compared. Besides, the structure–activity relationship of three different N-chemical configurations of BNCs in this photocatalytic degradation system was investigated, and the nitrogen species with photocatalytic activity were determined experimentally. The influence of photocatalyst dosage, presence of coexisting anions (Cl−, NO3 −, and SO4 2−) and initial solution pH on the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda (with optimal degradation activity) was investigated in detail. In addition, the reusability of BNC-eda with respect to the degradation of CBZ in four successive cycles was also studied. The results of this study were expected to provide new insights into the study of N-CQDs-modified Bi-based photocatalysts and provided technical support for the remediation of typical painkiller-contaminated water bodies.
The detailed reagent information of synthesized photocatalysts was shown in Text S1 of Supplementary Material.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , three NCs were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method. 5 mmol citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, followed by addition of 5 mmol urea or 335 μL L-tryptophan or 5 mmol ethylenediamine, respectively. The mixed solution was then added to a 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 ◦C for 5 h. Next, the transparent suspension was collected and purified in a 1000 molecular weight dialysis bag for 24 h. After rotary evaporation and freeze-drying, three NCs were obtained. They were named NC-ur, NC-trp and NC-eda, respectively.

Typically, composite catalysts were synthesized as follows: Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O (2 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of ethylene glycol by ultrasonication and stirring until complete dissolution. Then 20 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaBr solution was added, followed by 1, 2, 4, and 6 mL of prepared NCs (i.e. NC-ur, NC-trp, and NC-eda), respectively, and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 ◦C for 12 h. After natural cooling, the collected precipitate was washed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C. By adjusting the amount of different NC-x solutions (1, 2, 4, 6 mL), several complexes with different contents were synthesized. Finally, those materials with 4 mL (with best degradation activity for NC-eda modification) of NCs solution were selected as the main research objects, which were named BNC-ur, BNC-trp, BNC-eda, respectively.
BiOBr was also hydrothermally treated in the same procedure without adding any NCs according to the above steps.
The detailed characterization methods of synthesized photocatalysts were shown in Text S2 of Supplementary Material.

Carbamazepine (CBZ, >98 %, J&K Scientific Company of China) as a goal pollutant, the degradation ability of photocatalysts was evaluated. To achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium, photocatalysts (30 mg) and 10 mg/L CBZ (50 mL) were first stirred in the dark for 30 min in each CBZ degradation experiment. Then the 50 W LED visible-light lamp was turned on (dominant wavelength was 475 nm, and the background plot inserted in Fig. 5 (a) showed the detailed LED wavelength range). The suspension was collected at specified time intervals and collected through 0.22 μm membrane filters for subsequent testing. Data were presented as mean and standard deviation based on two replicates of the above experiments.

The photoelectrochemical tests of photocatalysts BiOBr, BNC-ur, BNC-trp, and BNC-eda were performed using the CS350H electrochemical workstation (Wuhan CorrTest Instruments Corp. Ltd). Additionally, details including recycle experiments, photoelectrochemical measurements and gas feeding experiments were described in Text S3 of Supplementary Material.
Analytical methods of CBZ concentration and ecotoxicity of CBZ photodegradation intermediates were presented in Text S4 of Supplementary Material.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of BiOBr and BNCs (Fig. 3 (a)) indicated a tetragonal structure (JCPDS no. 78–0348) and no different phases were detected, suggesting that the composites BNCs were pure in the crystalline phase despite the addition of NCs. It was due to the good dispersion of NCs in BiOBr surface, small size and low introduction content of NCs. Notably, the diffraction peak of BNC-eda (001) crystal plane was apparently weaker than those of pure BiOBr and other BNCs. It was reported that this may be related to the size reduction of BNC-eda along the crystallization direction of (001), the increase of absorption edges and the decrease of the band gap [38,39]. Furthermore, the diffraction peak of (110) crystal planes of BNC-eda was intense and acute, indicating that this was the preferred crystalline growth direction [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical composition and functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the functional groups and chemical composition of NCs, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed (Fig. 3(b)). In agreement with previous measurements, the FT-IR spectroscopy of the three NCs were almost identical. The absorption bands at around 1706, 1399, and 1189 cm−1 were attributed to the C=O, C=N, and C-NH-C stretching of the NCs, respectively [40]. The O-H stretching vibrational absorption peaks of NC-trp and NC-eda at 3417 cm−1 were red-shifted compared to the hydroxyl stretching vibrational absorption peak of NC-ur at 3213 cm−1 [41]. In addition, the N-H stretching vibration absorption peak of BNC-ur and BNC-eda was found at 1569 cm−1, the peak was not found in BNC-trp [42]. The N-H and C=N stretching vibrations of the NCs could be attributed to the O- and N-containing groups of the doped carbons, respectively, suggesting the successful introduction of N-atom functional groups. These results indicated that a variety of polar groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, amino) were present on the surface of NCs. Besides, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) of BiOBr and BNCs, the peaks at 516 and 1075 cm−1 could be attributed to the symmetrical stretching vibration peak of the Bi−O bond and Bi-Br bond [42]. The peaks at 1620 and 3452 cm−1 in BiOBr and BNCs could be related to the O−H vibration of adsorbed H2O [43]. Furthermore, the C=N vibrational absorption peaks corresponding to NCs at 1399 cm−1 were found in BNCs, proving the successful loading of NCs on BiOBr. The N doping could act as binding sites for coordination with Bi ions to improve the stability of BNCs [44]. However, in the FT-IR spectroscopy of BNCs, the peaks of NCs were obviously weakened or even disappeared. This could be because the functional groups of NCs bind to Bi atoms to form in situ growth sites, or because the size of BiOBr was much larger than that of NCs, resulting in partial coating of NCs in BiOBr [45].

Subsequently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the chemical state and surface elemental composition of the photocatalysts. According to the wide spectrum of the XPS (Fig. 3(d)), the identified elements were composed of NCs and BiOBr with a related composition. Fig. 4
showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of the detailed elements (C, O, N) of BNCs. All C 1s fine spectra of NCs could be fitted to three peaks (Fig. 4(a)), ascribed to the C-C/C=C, C-N, and O-C=O bond, respectively [42]. As shown in Fig. 4(b), all O 1s fine spectra of NCs could be fitted to three peaks: lattice oxygen of the Bi-O bond, oxygen vacancy and oxygen of chemisorbed hydroxides, respectively [43,46]. Due to the relatively low N doping, the peak intensity in the N 1s spectra of BNCs was weak (Fig. 4(c)), and it could only be roughly obtained that both BNC-ur and BNC-trp were composed of pyridine N (399.79 eV, 399.95 eV) and graphite N (401.18 eV), whereas only pyridine N (400.02 eV) was found in BNC-eda [47]. The percentage of N configuration in N doping could not be accurately obtained, so it was necessary to perform XPS analysis of NCs.

All NCs materials had distinct peaks only for C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s (Fig. 5(a)). High-resolution XPS spectra of the N 1s were analyzed (Fig. 5(b)), yielding NCs composed of different chemical states of N. The N 1s peaks of NC-ur, NC-trp, and NC-eda were composed of graphitic nitrogen (401.35 eV, 42.1 %) and pyrrole nitrogen (399.79 eV, 57.9 %), graphitic nitrogen (401.65 eV, 37.8 %) and pyrrole nitrogen (400.02 eV, 62.2 %), pyrrole nitrogen (400.13 eV, 100.0 %), respectively [47]. Interestingly, compared to BNCs, the N 1s peak in NCs composites shifted to a higher binding energy, indicating charge transfer from BiOBr to NCs. This illustrated the electron-rich nature and electron storage of NCs. All O 1s fine spectra of NCs could be fitted to two peaks (Fig. 5(c)), ascribed to the C=O and C-O bonds, respectively [48]. All C 1s fine spectra of NCs could be fitted to three peaks (Fig. 5(d)), ascribed to the C-C/C=C, C-N, and O-C=O bond respectively [42]. Fig. 5(e) described the surface element content of the NCs. The N atom percentages of NC-ur, NC-trp, and NC-eda were 5.54, 6.14, and 6.67, respectively, and the oxygen atom percentages were 23.66, 22.83, and 16.81, respectively. In addition, we analyzed the relative atomic ratio (Fig. 5(f)) of NCs. The trend of the O/C atomic ratio of NCs was opposite to that of the N/C value. Therefore, the decreased oxygen content and increased N content implied that the oxygen-containing defect site was exploited by N doping [49]. Besides, pH values of the NCs solutions varied slightly (Fig. S3) due to the different functional fractions of each precursor. These analyses showed that it was possible to effectively adjust the N chemical configuration and composition of NCs by using different precursor conditions in the pyrolysis self-assembly reaction of NCs.

The colors of BiOBr, BNC-ur, BNC-trp, and BNC-eda were shown from top to bottom in Fig. 6 (a). It could be clearly seen that the BNC-eda has the deepest color. Obviously, the lightest color BiOBr showed the strongest light absorption in the ultraviolet region with an absorption edge around 430 nm. The light absorption edge of BNC-ur and BNC-trp was similar to that of BiOBr, and the light absorption of BNC-ur and BNC-trp had no obvious improvement in the whole wavelength range. However, the absorption edge of BNC-eda was around 480 nm, which exhibited an obvious red shift compared to oher photocatalysts, and showed an enhanced absorption of light in the visible and in the near infrared region. This phenomenon indicated that the NC-eda modified BiOBr with more remarkable upconversion fluorescence performance could effectively enhance the visible-light capture, improving the photocatalytic performance.

More importantly, a bandgap narrowing was observed over the as-prepared BNC-eda. The band gap of photocatalysts were calculated from the Tauc’s plots using the formula Kubelka-Munk (calculation details were shown in Text S5 formula (2)) [50]. The band gaps of BiOBr, BNC-ur, BNC-trp, and BNC-eda were 2.44 eV, 2.46 eV, 2.35 eV, and 1.94 eV vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively (Fig. 6(b)). Interestingly, the band gap of these three composite photocatalysts decreased with increasing pyrrole nitrogen content.

To further investigate the energy band structure of BNC and pure BiOBr, Mott-Schottky (M−S) diagrams were analyzed at two frequencies (1.0 and 2.0 KHz). As shown in Fig. 6(c), the obvious positive slopes indicated that these photocatalysts are all n-type semiconductors. The conduction band (CB) position for n-type semiconductors could be approximately estimated from the flat band potentials found as the x-intercept in M−S plots [51]. Therefore, the CB potential could be calculated from the following equation: ENHE = EAg/AgCl + E0 (E0 = 0.197 eV), −0.70 eV, −0.82 eV, −0.92 eV, and −1.13 eV for BiOBr, BNC-ur, BNC-trp, and BNC-eda, respectively. Thus the energy band structure of the prepared photocatalyst can be displayed in Fig. 7 , and the VB potential was calculated from the sum of the band gap and CB potential. Obviously, after the modification of BiOBr by NCs with different nitrogen configurations, the positions of both CB and valence band (VB) of BNCs were also changed. However, the CB values of these BNCs all satisfied photocatalytic superoxide evolution thermodynamic conditions (e- + O2 = –O2 -, E0 = -0.33 eV vs. NHE). Therefore, the BNCs could be ideal photocatalysts with visible-light spectral response and appropriately tuned energy levels.

Photoelectrochemical tests, including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 6(d)) and transient photocurrent response (Fig. 6(e)), were performed in order to have a more detailed understanding of charge separation and transfer. It was shown that the photoelectrochemical properties of the NCs modified composite photocatalysts were all superior to those of pure BiOBr. As expected, BNC-eda showed the smallest Nyquist curve radius and the highest photocurrent response than other photocatalysts, demonstrating the low interfacial charge transfer impedance and high charge transfer rate of BNC-eda [52]. Besides, higher pyrrole nitrogen content in BNCs, smaller Nyquist curve radius and higher photocurrent intensity showed that the charge transfer impedance of BNCs doped with pyrrole N was greatly reduced and the charge transfer efficiency was improved. Additionally, it was discovered that the I-t curve was decaying, proving that the photogenerated charge carriers were recombining at the electrode's surface rather than participating in the oxidation of the water. Therefore, the proportion of recombined photogenerated holes would be larger the faster the decay occurs. Calculations might be made to determine the impact of charge accumulation on the surfaces of the photocatalysts (text S5, formula (3)). The results showed that the IC of BNCs was larger than BiOBr, reaching 0.224, 0.289, 0.321, and 0.499 for BiOBr, BNC-ur, BNC-trp, and BNC-eda, respectively, corresponding with the PCR and photodegradation activity results. This also showed that the photogenerated carriers of pure BiOBr were significantly recombined, which might be due to the heavy metal effect of Bi atom [53]. In this way, NCs served as a mediator in the BiOBr photocatalytic process, providing a transport channel for photogenerated electrons. In the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve, the stronger electron transfer ability of BNCs corresponded to the higher current density. When BiOBr was employed alone, it was evident that the current density was quite low, instead BNC-eda exhibited the maximum current density (Fig. S4).

The photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate of the prepared photocatalysts was examined by photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 6(f)). A lower electron-hole complexation rate and more photocatalytic activity were demonstrated by the reduced fluorescence emission intensity [54,55]. When excited at a wavelength of 395 nm, the PL emission spectra of pristine BiOBr in displayed a strong emission peak at 432 nm. The photoluminescence intensity of these BNCs at 432 nm was weaker than BiOBr, suggesting a remarkable promotion of charge transfer and suppression of the electron-hole complexation rate by the introduction of NCs. In particular, the emission peak of BNC-eda showed a noticeable redshift from 432 nm to 436 nm. This could be related to its smaller band gap [56]. Additionally, the PL peak of BNC-eda at 436 nm was significantly weaker than that of BNC-trp and BNC-ur. The improved electron transfer in BNCs could be attributed to the electron absorption ability of pyrrole-N atoms in the conjugated C-plane [57,58]. This further suggested that the introduction of pyrrole-N led to a significant improvement in electron transfer in the BNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by various BCNs
ce:section.ce:para: The visible-light driven photocatalytic activities of the as-fabricated BCNs and pure BiOBr for the removal of CBZ were presented in Fig. 8 (a). Almost no adsorption was observed between CBZ and photocatalyst within 30 min of dark conditions. And no decrease in CBZ concentration was observed when the LED visible-light was turned on in the absence of photocatalyst. Therefore, the degradation of CBZ by this system was attributed to the contribution of the catalysts under illumination. Specifically, the removal efficiencies of BiOBr and BNCs for CBZ in 15 min shown in Fig. 8(b) were 55.2 %, 59.2 %, 73.7 %, and 99.6 %, respectively. In addition, BNC-eda showed the highest photocatalytic activity, as the pseudo-first-order rate constant k for BNC-eda (k = 0.363 min−1) was over 5 times more than that of pure BiOBr (k = 0.072 min−1). In addition, BNC-eda also exhibited superior photodegradation performance toward CBZ than other reported photocatalysts (Table S1). Meanwhile, composite photocatalysts synthesis with different volumes (1, 2, and 6 mL) of NCs were also subjected to photocatalytic degradation of CBZ (Fig. S5(a-c)). It could be seen that the degradation performance of all composites for CBZ was improved compared with pure BiOBr. In addition, the degradation rate trend of the three groups of photocatalysts synthesized at these three volumes was consistent with that of BNCs-4 system. More importantly, the pseudo-first-order constants k of CBZ photodegradation by these four groups of photocatalysts BNCs-1/2/4/6 were linearly positively relationship well (R2 > 99 %) with the pyrrole N in the corresponding NCs, respectively (Fig. 8(c) and Fig. S5(d-f)). Combined with the above experimental results, it was easy to draw an important conclusion that the improvement of photocatalytic performance in these systems might depend only on pyrrole N rather than graphite N. Within a certain range of NCs addition, with the increase of pyrrole N content (%) in the same volume of NCs modified BiOBr, the pseudo-first-order constant k of CBZ under visible-light also increased. Analyzing the reasons, on the one hand, the modification of NCs could improve both the visible-light absorption and the separation of h+ and e-. On the other hand, pyrrole N could enhance the adsorption of oxygen and reacted the stored electrons with it to form more active species for CBZ degradation [36]. Therefore, the photocatalytic activities of BNC-eda were obviously higher than pure BiOBr and other BNCs under visible-light.

Interestingly, as the volume of NC-eda was increased to 4 mL, the pseudo-first-order constant k of BNC-eda reached its maximum value, but excess NC-eda (6 mL) resulted in a significant decrease in k. This phenomenon could be related to the fact that as the amount of NCs increased, producing more exposed active sites, an excessive amount of NCs would have a shielding effect and inhibit photocatalysis [59], which reduced the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, the optimal amount of NCs added to BNC-eda was 4 mL, and considering the economy of composites in fully utilizing NCs, the amount of NCs added was set to 4 mL in the subsequent study.

BNC-eda was selected for the dosage experiment (Fig. 8(d)). The removal effectiveness of CBZ under visible-light irradiation might be improved by increasing the dosage of BNC-eda from 0.4 g/L to 0.6 g/L. However, increasing the dose of BNC-eda to 1.0 g/L slightly reduced the efficiency of CBZ elimination. This was because as the dosage increased, the number of active radicals produced in the solution increased and the removal efficiency of CBZ became higher. However, when the dosage of BNC-eda was higher than 0.8 g/L, there was increased optical scattering and hindered optical absorption. In addition, pseudo-first-order constant k of CBZ were similar at 0.6 g/L (k = 0.363 min−1) and 0.8 g/L (k = 0.371 min−1). Therefore, based on the economic benefits, a dosage of 0.6 g/L was selected for the systems. To determine whether the differences in photocatalytic activity among BNCs were generalized, the photocatalytic removal efficiencies of BNCs for different organic pollutants such as rhodamine B (Rh B) and bisphenol-A (BPA) were compared. Fig. 9 (a) showed that BiOBr-eda still exhibited the highest removal efficiency, confirming its excellent photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of inorganic anions and pH on degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda
ce:section.ce:para: Since inorganic anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 –) were usually present in real waters, the effect of their presence on CBZ photodegradation had to be investigated in detail. To avoid the potential influence of cations, sodium salts were used for the photocatalytic process. These three ions were added to the CBZ solution at 1 mM. As shown in the Fig. S6, there was a significant inhibition of CBZ photodegradation by SO4 2- and Cl- at this concentration. This could be due to the fact that SO4 2- and Cl- not only competed with CBZ for the same reaction sites on the photocatalyst surface, but could also react with h+ to form less reactive substances, thus affecting the photocatalytic activity of BNC-eda [59,60]. However, this concentration of NO3 – has almost no effect on the photodegradation. Therefore, HNO3 was used to adjust the pH effect in subsequent experiments.

As shown in Fig. S7, pH significantly affected the degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda. The photocatalytic degradation performance was reduced with the raise of initial pH. For BNC-eda, the point of zero charge (pHpzc) was around 8.0 (Fig. S8), indicating that its surface was positively charged at pH < pHpzc and negatively charged at pH > pHpzc. As depicted in Fig. S9, CBZ had two pKa values, namely 2.3 and 13.9, so CBZ existed in aqueous solution in the form of zwitterionic ions in the range of pH 3–11 [61]. The highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency was achieved at pH 3, which could be attributed to the structure of –CONH2 in CBZ being more susceptible to degradation under acidic conditions [62]. When the pH of solution increased to alkaline conditions (pH = 9,10; pH > pHpzc), the photocatalytic showed a poor degradation efficiency. This is because under alkaline conditions, electrostatic repulsion could occur between the negatively charged photocatalysts and the electron-rich aromatic nucleus of the CBZ molecule [63,64].

The recycling experiments for the photodegradation of CBZ were carried out with four reused cycles to evaluate the stability of the BNC-eda under visible-light. As shown in Fig. S10 (a), only 75.4 % of CBZ could be degraded by BNC-eda within 15 min, which was obviously lower than the first round (99.8 %). However, the degradation efficiency of BiOBr remained almost no decline after four cycles (Fig. S10(b)). In addition, FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S10(c)) showed that there was no significant difference between the samples before and after the photocatalytic reaction. The XRD pattern (Fig. S10(d)) of the above samples before and after four cycles showed no obvious difference. As shown in the Fig. S11, the Bi 4f [65] and Br 3d [66] peaks, C 1s and O 1s peaks of BNC-eda after the reaction were basically unchanged compared with those before the reaction, indicating that the BNC-eda showed relatively stable performance and crystal structure. However, in the N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of BNC-eda and BNC-eda 4th (Fig. S11 (b)), not only the peak position was slightly shifted, but also surface N content decreased from 2.67 % to 1.61 %. In summary, therefore, the decrease in the stability of BNC-eda could be attributed to the shedding of NC-eda. Although the degradation efficiency of BNC-eda gradually decreased in four cycles, its excellent photodegradation efficiency and unchanged crystal structure also suggested that there was value in future research on BNC-eda [67].

In order to understand the predominate free radicals of photodegradation of CBZ by BiOBr and BNCs, benzoquinone (BQ), acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), and furfuryl alcohol (FFA) were used as the scavengers of •O2 –, h+, •OH, and 1O2 to study their effects, respectively (Fig. 9(b-e)). The addition of BQ significantly reduced the removal efficiency of CBZ in the BiOBr and BNCs reaction systems, proving that •O2 – played a crucial role in CBZ removal. After EDTA-2Na was added into the BiOBr, BNC-ur, BNC-trp, and BNC-eda reaction systems, the degradation efficiency of CBZ decreased to 9.72 %, 15.36 %, 12.00 %, and 86.48 %, respectively. Obviously, the inhibition of BNC-eda was much weaker than other reaction systems, which could be attributed to the strong •O2 – production ability of BNC-eda. On the contrary, the addition of IPA had no significant effect on the degradation of CBZ in BiOBr and BNCs, indicating that •OH played a minor role in the reaction system. Interestingly, the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by all three BNCs was suppressed to some extent after the addition of FFA, suggesting the possible generation of 1O2 in BNCs systems. Therefore, it was necessary to further explore radicals in these reaction systems. An electron spin response (ESR) spectroscopy analysis was performed to investigate the photocatalytic process of BNCs. The 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-Noxide (DMPO) was used as the spin-trapping agent to detect •O2 – and •OH (Fig. 9(g, h)), and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMP) was used as trapping agent to characterize the 1O2 (Fig. 9(i)). The intensity of DMPO-•O2 – of BNC-eda was much stronger than BNC-ur and BNC-trp. Besides, a similar phenomenon was observed in the 1O2 trapping test, which demonstrated the generation of 1O2, and it could be inferred that 1O2 may be generated by the reaction of •O2 – and h+, in agreement with the previous report [59]. However, no signal peak of hydroxyl group was detected under visible light, which was consistent with the radical trapping experiments and the energy band results. Therefore, the conclusion was that the active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by BNCs were •O2 –, h+, and 1O2. However, the radical that played a major role in the BNC-eda reaction system was only •O2 –, while in the other reaction systems it was •O2 – and h+. Radical quenching experiments and ESR results indicated that different N chemical configurations could modulate the generation of radicals for the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by BNCs, with BNC-eda containing 100 % pyrrole N generating the most •O2 – and 1O2.

Since both reduction and oxidation occurred concurrently in photocatalysis, radicals could be generated from the oxidation of H2O or the reduction of O2. To investigate whether O2 was involved in the BNC-eda reaction system, separate experiments were conducted with N2 and O2. Fig. 9(f) showed that the CBZ photocatalytic degradation performance of BNC-eda was almost completely inhibited under N2 atmosphere. And when O2 was introduced into the reaction system, the CBZ degradation efficiency of BNC-eda was enhanced. Therefore, the •O2 – generated in the BNC-eda photocatalytic reaction system originated from the reduction of O2.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of BNCs was proposed (Scheme 2 ). Under visible light excitation, photogenerated electron-hole pairs were generated in BiOBr with a suitable band gap. NCs could be used as electron transfer intermediates, and electrons on CB of BiOBr were rapidly transferred to NCs, so that photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be effectively separated. The electrons reacted with the dissolved oxygen captured by the reacting system to produce a large amount of •O2 –. Then a portion of •O2 – was oxidized by h+ to form 1O2. Subsequently h+, •O2 –, and 1O2 acted as reactive radicals produced by BNCs to degrade CBZ. Finally, regarding BNC-eda having the best CBZ photodegradation performance, it could be attributed to the doping of NC-eda with the highest pyrrole N percentage content (100 %). This greatly modified the morphology of BNC-eda, enhanced visible light absorption, and acted as an electron enrichment center to enhance the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs resulting in the generation of more •O2 – and 1O2 radicals.

High-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) was used to detect the transformation products of the BNC-eda photocatalytic degradation of CBZ. The m/z values, molecular structures, and mass spectra of the ten identified intermediates were shown in Fig. S12. There were two probable photocatalytic degradation pathways of CBZ were proposed in Fig. 10. First, P1 (0,11-dihydro-10,11-epoxy-carbamazepine) was formed when CBZ was epoxidized, making it susceptible to assault by active compounds [68]. Then, P3 (10,11-dihydroxy-carbamazepine) was produced by hydroxylation. P5 (4-formyl-9-oxoacridine-10(9H)-carboxamide) might be created at the two –OH sites of P3 through heterocyclic ring opening. Subsequently, P5 was further transformed into P7 (N-(2-(hydroxy(phenyl) methyl) phenyl) formamide). Finally, the pyrolysis of aldehyde and amine/carboxyl groups allowed P7 to be changed into P8 (acridone). In another pathway, middle seven-membered heterocyclic ring of CBZ molecules could be attacked by electrons and holes to generate isomers P2 (acridone-N-carbaldehyde) and P4 (4-aldehyde-9-ari done) [69]. Subsequently, intermediates could be oxidized to product P6 (acridine) by •O2 –, losing ketone and aldehyde groups. P6 was further hydroxylated and oxidized to P8(acridone) [70]. Intermediate P4 could then be oxidized to product P8 (acridin-9(10H)-one), losing ketone and aldehyde groups. In the presence of the active species, the above products would undergo further redox and ring-opening reactions to form the major ring-opening products P9 (acridin-9(10H)-one) and P10 (benzoic acid).

Three-dimension excitation-emission matrices (3D EEM) was used to examine the changes in CBZ during the photocatalytic degradation process in the BNC-eda system. Six samples were collected after photocatalytic reaction of BNC-eda under visible-light irradiation at 0 min, 1 min, 3 min, 5 min, 10 min, 15 min. As shown in Fig. 11(a-f), no obvious fluorescence signal was detected in the initial CBZ. However, the fluorescence peaks at λEx/λEm = (300–400 nm)/(400–500 nm) and λEx/λEm = (200–300 nm)/(400–550 nm) gradually appeared after visible-light irradiation, and reached the strongest at 3 min, which was attributed to the formation of humic acid-like organic matters and fulvic acids [71]. This was due to the degradation of CBZ into small molecules as well as the mineralization of the aromatic fraction and conjugated heterocyclic intermediates during photodegradation [72]. The intensity of the fluorescence peak decreased significantly when the visible light irradiation time was increased to 15 min, especially for humic acid-like organic matters, indicating the formation of non-fluorescent substances. In addition, as shown in the Fig. S13, the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda achieved a TOC removal rate of 34.5 % within 15 min, and the TOC removal rate could reach 72.6 % by extending the reaction time to 70 min. Therefore, it was reasonable to infer that CBZ and its intermediates might be fully degraded and mineralized by extending the reaction time.

During the degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda, it was necessary to analyze the toxicity of the intermediates. Utilizing the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.), the mutagenicity, developmental toxicity, and bioaccumulation factor of CBZ and its intermediates were assessed based on the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) analysis [73,74]. The majority of the compounds, as seen in Fig. 12 (a), had negative mutagenicity, suggesting that the oxidation reaction might lessen the mutagenicity of CBZ by BNC-eda. The degradation products P6, P8, P9, and P10 were even regarded as developmental non-toxicants while CBZ was a developmental toxicant and the oxidation process lowered the toxicity of several of the degradation products (Fig. 12(b)). The bioaccumulation factor of most of the intermediates was reduced (Fig. 12(c)), the only exceptions being P2, P6, P7, and P8. Thus, the mutagenicity, developmental toxicity, and bioaccumulation factors of the final degradation by-products (P9, P10) were significantly lower compared to CBZ. Obviously, photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda could significantly reduce its environmental risks.

In summary, we prepared three NCs by hydrothermal method with different precursors and then decorated BiOBr, respectively, which resulted in the changes in the elemental compositions (N/C and N/O ratios) and N-bond configurations (Ngra, Npyrr) of BNCs. The differences in the chemical configurations of NCs strongly affected the photoelectric performance, energy band structure and surface morphology of BNCs. Among them the graded microflower BNC-eda composed of the thinnest nanosheets exhibited the strongest visible-light absorption, the highest photocurrent density, the lowest charge transfer resistance, and the most negative conduction band. In short, NCs significantly improved the light trapping efficiency and charge separation of photocatalysts. The pseudo-first-order rate constant k = 0.363 min−1 for BNC-eda was over 5 times more than that of pure BiOBr (k = 0.072 min−1). The k of BNCs photodegradation CBZ was linearly positively related to the percentage content of pyrrole N in the corresponding NCs with r2 = 0.9999, respectively, suggesting that the enhancement of photocatalytic activity depended only on the doping of pyrrole N rather than graphite N. In addition, BNC-eda doped with 100 % pyrrole N had the strongest DMPO-•O2 – and TEMP-1O2 intensity compared to BNC-trp and BNC-ur, where both of •O2 – and 1O2 were important reactive oxygen species for the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ by BNCs. Besides, the pyrrole N as the reactive active site for the degradation of CBZ by BNC-eda was confirmed by cycling experiments and XPS analysis. Finally, according to the experimental and theoretical results, the possible degradation pathways and toxicity analysis of BNC-eda to CBZ were clarified. In conclusion, this work not only provided a new idea for the design of N-CQDs modified Bi-based photocatalysts, but also synthesized highly efficient BNC-eda to provide a new option for the removal of CBZ from water under visible-light irradiation.
